column1: # Column: english_word — case-insensitive match against collection_words.english_word
# Column: context      — short label (max 100 chars): idiom
column3: politeness

column2: max 200 chars; stored as plain text

column3: politeness-sensitive words
note
Essential in English requests; omitting it can sound rude. British speakers use it more frequently than Americans in everyday transactions like ordering food.
British English uses 'sorry' far more than most languages — as apology
column5: and even to attract attention. Americans tend to say 'excuse me' in the same situations.

column3: Always expected after a service or gift in English-speaking cultures. 'Cheers' is a common informal British substitute. 'Thanks' is neutral; 'ta' is very informal British.
Used to get past someone (British prefer 'sorry')
column5: or introduce a polite interruption. Americans also use it after burping; British say 'pardon'.

column3: British English uses 'pardon?' to ask someone to repeat themselves. Americans say 'sorry?' or 'excuse me?' in the same context. Considered more formal than 'what?'
Primarily a drinking toast worldwide
column4: but in British English it also means 'thank you' and 'goodbye'. A tourist using it as only a toast may seem overly formal to British locals.

column3: The British are famous for orderly queuing. 'Jumping the queue' is considered very rude. Americans say 'line' or 'stand in line'. The word itself marks British speech.
Means two weeks. Widely used in Britain
column5: and Australia but almost unknown in American English. A distinctly British-flavoured word learners often miss.

column3: The public house is a central social institution in British culture — not just a bar. Buying rounds for your group is an expected social ritual; refusing your turn can cause offence.
In British English chips are thick-cut fried potatoes (like French fries). In American English chips are thin crisps. 'Crisps' is the British word for the thin
In British English 'pants' means underwear; trousers are the outer garment. American English uses 'pants' for trousers. Using the wrong one can cause embarrassing misunderstandings.
In British English a biscuit is a sweet or savoury dry snack (cookie or cracker). In American English a biscuit is a soft
column4: flaky bread roll served warm with meals.

column3: American English uses 'fall' for the season that British English calls 'autumn'. Both forms are understood globally
column4: but 'autumn' sounds more formal in American contexts.

column3: The American English word for the water tap. British English says 'tap'. 'Faucet' sounds technical or formal to British ears; 'tap' sounds quaint to Americans.
British English for an elevator. 'Lift' is also widely used in Irish and Australian English. American tourists often miss signs saying 'lift' in British buildings.
British English for an apartment. 'Flat' is standard in Britain
column5: and much of the Commonwealth. American English prefers 'apartment'; 'flat' sounds distinctly British.

column3: British English for a mobile phone (cell phone). 'Mobile' emphasises portability; 'cell' refers to the cellular network technology. Both terms are understood globally.
Theatre superstition: wishing 'good luck' before a performance is considered bad luck
column4: so 'break a leg' is used instead. Now used broadly to mean 'good luck' in any high-stakes situation.

column3: Something very easy to do. Possibly from the tradition of giving cake as a prize in competitions. Used across all English-speaking countries; avoid in formal or written contexts.
To study hard. Widely used in American English among students. British equivalents include 'hit the books' or 'swot up'. Informal; not suitable for formal writing.
Feeling slightly ill or unwell. Thought to originate from sailors who sheltered below deck in bad weather when sick. Common in both British and American English.
To endure a painful or difficult situation with determination. Originally referred to soldiers biting a bullet during surgery before anaesthesia. Used across all varieties of English.

column1: cost an arm and a leg
column3: To be very expensive. Emphasises that the price is unreasonably high. Common in informal spoken and written English in Britain
To stop trying; to surrender or abandon an effort. This is one of the most common phrasal verbs in English. The meaning changes completely if 'up' is removed: 'give' alone means to donate.
Has two distinct meanings: to search for information ('look up a word in a dictionary') and to improve ('things are looking up'). Context makes the meaning clear.
To encounter unexpectedly ('I came across an old letter') or to create an impression ('she comes across as confident'). Both senses are common in everyday English.
To yield or surrender after resistance. Different from 'give up'
column4: which implies abandoning a task entirely. 'Give in' suggests capitulating to pressure from others.

column3: To postpone or delay ('she put off the meeting') or to cause dislike/reluctance ('the smell put me off'). A highly productive phrasal verb with multiple common senses.
To refuse an offer or to reduce the volume or intensity of something ('turn down the music'). The refusal sense is more common in interpersonal contexts.
When an aircraft departs
column5: or someone removes clothing. One of the most polysemous phrasal verbs in English; learners need to acquire all three senses.

column3: To exhaust a supply ('we ran out of milk') or to expire ('time ran out'). Very common in everyday conversation; no single-word synonym covers both senses as naturally.
To raise a child or to mention a topic in conversation. The child-rearing sense is more formal than 'raise'. Both senses are frequent in everyday British and American English.
To establish or arrange something ('set up a meeting'; 'set up a company'). Also used in the sense of framing someone for a crime ('he was set up'). Extremely common in business English.
British English for having a good relationship ('we get on well') and also for progress ('how are you getting on?'). Americans prefer 'get along'. Common in British everyday speech.
Using 'would' instead of 'will' adds politeness in requests. 'Would you help me?' is considerably softer than 'Will you help me?' This modal is central to polite English interaction.
British English uses 'shall' for first-person future ('I shall return') and polite offers ('Shall I carry that?'). American English rarely uses 'shall'; 'will' and 'should' cover the same ground.
A formal or emphatic word for 'yes' or to reinforce a statement. British English uses it more frequently than American English. Overusing it in casual speech can sound stiff.
In British English 'quite' means 'fairly' or 'completely' depending on context. In American English it almost always means 'very'. This difference causes frequent misunderstanding.
Used as a general expression of approval or enthusiasm in British English (equivalent to 'great' or 'awesome'). In other contexts it retains the meaning of exceptional intelligence or brightness.
Informal British and Australian English for 'think' or 'suppose' ('I reckon it'll rain'). Americans find it old-fashioned or rural. Very common in casual British everyday speech.
As a verb
column4: British English uses 'fancy' to mean 'want' or 'feel like' ('Do you fancy a cup of tea?') and 'to be attracted to' ('she fancies him'). Americans use it only as an adjective meaning elaborate.

column3: Informal British English for a friend. Used as a term of address ('alright mate?'). Australian English also uses it broadly. Americans rarely use this sense; 'buddy' or 'pal' are closer equivalents.
British informal for 'real'
column5: or 'thorough' ('a proper meal'; 'that was proper scary'). Used as an adverb intensifier in Northern English dialects. Americans would say 'real' or 'actual'.

column3: A very common British intensifier ('bloody good'; 'bloody hell'). Considered mildly offensive by older generations but widely used in informal speech. Essentially absent from American English.
British English for 'garbage'
column5: or 'of poor quality'. 'That's rubbish!' dismisses a claim. American English prefers 'trash'

column3: Slightly irreverent behaviour in a good-natured way ('a cheeky smile'; 'a cheeky pint'). Positive or neutral in British usage; largely unknown in American English.
British English uses 'hire' for short-term temporary use ('hire a car'; 'hire a hall'). American English uses 'rent' for the same concept. 'Hire' in American English mainly means to employ someone.
British English for a vacation ('we're going on holiday'). American English uses 'vacation' for trips and 'holiday' only for festive days like Christmas or Thanksgiving.
The rear storage compartment of a car. Americans call it a 'trunk'. British English also uses 'boot' for a type of footwear
column4: which matches the American meaning. Context distinguishes the two.

column3: The hinged cover over a car's engine. Americans call it the 'hood'. British English also uses 'bonnet' for a traditional hat
column4: so context is essential when discussing cars.

column3: The British term for a high-speed multi-lane road. Americans call this a 'highway' or 'freeway'; Australians say 'freeway' or 'motorway'. In Britain 'M' followed by a number names specific motorways.
British English for the footpath beside a road (Americans say 'sidewalk'). In American English 'pavement' means the road surface itself. Using the wrong word causes confusion.
The preferred British and formal word for the season after summer. American English favours 'fall'. Both words are understood internationally but mark the speaker's variety of English.
In formal British English 'pudding' means dessert generally
column4: not only the specific steamed or boiled dish. 'What's for pudding?' means 'What's for dessert?' This usage confuses many non-British learners.
